narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an impressive LSAT score of 42—both above the typical averages for students in this dataset—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your upper-middle family income background and full-time study plans. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 62.6% probability that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the program. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The most influential was your undergraduate GPA; even though it's stronger than average, it still pushed the model toward predicting a higher chance of bar failure compared to students with even higher GPAs who typically pass. Next, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 9 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it signals solid late-stage academic performance relative to peers, countering some of the risk. Your family income bracket came third, contributing toward the failure prediction, as the model associates upper-middle incomes with slightly elevated bar failure rates in patterns seen across past students. Overall, while your LSAT and Year 1 decile of 8 are strengths above average, they weren't enough to offset the top factors' combined push toward risk. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile aligns more closely with those predicted to struggle on the bar, particularly due to that GPA influence despite your other highs. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats to protect the school's reputation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score or bolstering your GPA narrative with explanations of upward trends like your strong Year 3 decile. You might also target schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and background more flexibly. Gathering letters from professors highlighting your decile improvements could help reframe your story for the next application cycle. With targeted improvements, your above-average markers position you well to flip this prediction.
